witnesses.description: Exact closed rational interval sum and product around one, including the second-order product-width term.

checks.claim: The listed input, sum, and product intervals are closed rational intervals with the listed widths.
checks.notes: The validator checks endpoint order, closedness, positivity, and width arithmetic.

checks.claim: The sum interval is the exact endpoint-wise sum of the two input intervals.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes lower(X)+lower(Y) and upper(X)+upper(Y).

checks.claim: Because both inputs are nonnegative, the product interval is the exact endpoint product.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes lower(X)*lower(Y) and upper(X)*upper(Y).

checks.claim: The product upper bound exceeds the linearized shortcut by the second-order width product.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes interval widths, width(X)*width(Y), and product_upper - linearized_product_upper.

checks.claim: The product interval upper endpoint is at most 5001/5000.
checks.notes: Exact interval replay computes product_upper = 100020001/100000000, so the shortcut upper bound 5001/5000 is rejected; the separate qf-lra-bad-interval-product-upper row owns the checked Farkas evidence.

checks.claim: The malformed interval product upper-bound claim is linearly inconsistent.

data.certificate: The resource regression parses the source SMT-LIB artifact, emits UnsatFarkas evidence, and independently checks the certificate.

checks.notes: The source QF_LRA artifact isolates the scalar upper-bound conflict after exact interval replay computes the product endpoint.

checks.claim: General interval arithmetic, dependency management, outward rounding for floating-point endpoints, and interval-analysis soundness theorems require future proof or numerical-honesty resources.

data.target_theorem_shape: For arbitrary rational or floating-point intervals and an expression language, prove sound interval enclosures under explicit rounding and dependency assumptions.
data.future_checker: Lean theorem statements for exact interval arithmetic plus QF_FP or bit-vector routes for concrete floating-point outward rounding.

checks.notes: The finite exact row is a checked resource for one rational interval product, not a theorem about interval packages or hardware floating point.
